lede: Suep Cata (life-extension rite) accounts for 21 catalogued manuscripts, 20 of them flagged research-priority. It clusters in Nan (48% of the corpus for this subject), ahead of Phayao and Chiang Mai. Nearly all (100%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (81%) over palm-leaf · ใบลาน (19%). Dated witnesses run 1902–1939 CE (3 of 21 carry a date).

authored.title: Suep Cata (life-extension rite)
authored.body_html:
What this is

Suep cata (สืบชะตา — "to prolong the fate") is the great life-extension ceremony of the north: the rite that lengthens a threatened lifespan, renews waning fortune and wards off a fate the horoscope has read as failing. It is one of the most characteristic and still most widely performed of Lanna rituals, and the catalogue holds 21 witnesses whose titles name it. Small in count, it is outsized in importance — 20 of the 21 are flagged research-priority (95%), the densest priority-share of any subject the scorer touches.

Where divination becomes action

Suep cata is the hinge at which reading fate turns into changing it. The titles put the two operations side by side — "Suep anyu, Poet cata": open the horoscope (poet cata), then extend the life (suep anyu). The astrologer's holasat diagnoses the crisis; the suep cata rite is the intervention. This is the clearest single answer in the collection to what the saiyasat is for: not merely to know the future but to act on it, to buy back time against a bad reckoning.

And it works at every scale. The manuscripts prescribe suep cata for a single person, for a monk alone (Pap suep cata phikkhu ton diao), for the rice (Suep cata khao), and — strikingly — for the whole town: Suep cata ban mueang, the collective life-extension of the muang itself. The same ritual logic that renews one body renews the body politic.

The shape of the collection

It is a working ritual manual on paper — 17 mulberry-paper against 4 palm-leaf — barely dated (3 witnesses, 1902–1939) and late, exactly the profile of a text in active use rather than archival storage. It clusters in Nan (10) and Phayao (6), scattered across small vernacular temples with no dominant library — the wichaa survival pattern, the opposite of the canon's Wat Sung Men gravity.

Notes

  • It anchors the whole ritual kit. Suep cata rarely stands alone: the bundles bind it to su khwan soul-calling, sut thon recitation, holasat and offering (Suep cata khao, ... Kam sut thon; Kam su khwan nya, Kam suep cata). To read one suep cata manuscript is to read a practitioner's entire working repertoire for a life-crisis.
  • The astrology/magic seam runs straight through it. It sits under both astrology (8) and magic & ritual (12) because it is both — diagnosis and cure in one rite. It is the best worked example of why those two genres cannot be cleanly separated.
  • A living tradition, thinly recorded — a prime crawl target. Near-total priority-density and still performed today, yet under-represented in the digitized record: suep cata is exactly the material this archive should document first. Any active practitioner's suep cata text is a primary source of the first rank.
